content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Admissions and intake-equity regulation (keep structure, fix the gate)'. Given Hungary's already-highest-in-EU school segregation, any policy alternative to age 10-12 korai szelekció should be judged primarily by whether it reduces SES-based sorting at intake, not only by whether it preserves the apparent ~10% pedagogical value-added advantage for admitted students.
opportunities: Reduced SES-skew in admitted cohorts at the 6/8-year gate, addressing the documented near-zero intake share of multiply-disadvantaged pupils.
Preserves the approximately 10% competency-gain advantage associated with 6/8-year tracks for admitted students, since the track structure itself is untouched.
strengths: Hungarian 6/8-year gimnázium students show roughly 10% larger competency gains than similar-ability, similar-background peers in 4-year gimnázium, per new statistical-model research controlling for family background.
Only about 8% of a school-age cohort attends 6- or 8-year gimnázium tracks, making this a small but disproportionately contested segment of the system.
threats: Implementation by ministerial decree rather than cardinal law leaves the mechanism exposed to reversal by a subsequent government within a single electoral cycle; the scenario does not propose a specific entrenchment design (e.g., cardinal-law status, cross-party pact, or sunset-clause safeguard) to mitigate this.
Fidesz's demonstrated framing of any selection-related change as ideologically totalitarian creates reversal risk even for a narrower admissions-only intervention, not only for structural abolition.
weaknesses: Whether the ~10% value-added estimate reflects genuine school effect versus unmeasured peer-composition or teacher-sorting confounds remains unresolved.
The magnitude of church-school funding advantage (if any) relative to state schools remains contested and unresolved by available sources.
